user_query:
I need a finance follow-up report from my universal chat app’s history. Read these exported files under input/:
- input/threads.json (list of conversation threads with participants)
- input/messages.jsonl (one JSON message per line across all threads)
- input/search_results.json (precomputed full-text search hits for finance-related terms)

Build three outputs under output/finance/:
1) output/finance/summary.json — For every thread that has at least one qualifying finance-related hit, produce a JSON array of thread summaries. Each summary must include:
   - thread_id (string)
   - thread_name (string)
   - participants (array of unique non-bridge display_name strings, sorted case-insensitively)
   - first_match_ts (ISO 8601 timestamp of the earliest qualifying matched message in that thread)
   - last_match_ts (ISO 8601 timestamp of the latest qualifying matched message in that thread)
   - matched_messages_count (integer, count of qualifying matched messages in that thread)
   - keyword_frequency (object mapping each lowercase term to its count within that thread)
   - total_messages_considered (integer, count of non-system, non-bridge messages in that thread across the entire dataset)
   Sort the array by thread_name (case-insensitive ascending).

2) output/finance/top_threads.csv — The top 5 threads by matched_messages_count (descending; tie-break by last_match_ts descending, then thread_id ascending). Include a header row and exactly these columns:
   thread_id,thread_name,matched_messages_count,first_match_ts,last_match_ts,participants_count
   Where participants_count equals the length of participants for that thread (as defined in summary.json).

3) output/finance/contexts.jsonl — One JSON object per qualifying matched message, sorted by match_timestamp ascending, then thread_id ascending, then message_id ascending. Each object must include:
   - thread_id (string)
   - message_id (string)
   - keyword (lowercase chosen term; see selection rules below)
   - match_timestamp (ISO 8601 timestamp of the matched message)
   - context_before (array of up to 2 prior non-system, non-bridge messages from the same thread, ordered oldest-to-newest)
   - context_after (array of up to 1 subsequent non-system, non-bridge message from the same thread, ordered oldest-to-newest)
   Each message object in context_before/context_after must contain: id, timestamp (ISO 8601), sender_display_name, and text.

Definition of a qualifying matched message and rules:
- Terms to consider (case-insensitive): "invoice", "payment", "paid", "wire", "net 30", "po", "purchase order".
- Treat input/search_results.json as authoritative for which messages are hits: only messages listed there count as matched. If a message_id appears under multiple terms, assign the keyword using these precedence rules: choose the term with the greatest number of characters; if still tied, choose lexicographically ascending.
- Exclude any matched message if the message has is_system = true or its sender.is_bridge = true in input/messages.jsonl; such messages must not appear in any output.
- Exclude system or bridge messages from participants, from total_messages_considered, and from context_before/context_after. Deduplicate any duplicate message entries in the search results so each qualifying message_id appears exactly once in contexts.jsonl.
- All timestamps must remain in their original ISO 8601 format from input/messages.jsonl.

Notes:
- Use input/threads.json to get thread metadata and the participants list (filter out bridge participants when building the participants array).
- Use input/messages.jsonl to pull message bodies, senders, and timestamps, and to construct contexts.
- Use input/search_results.json only to identify which messages are finance-related; do not re-run text search.
- Write only the three files above under output/finance/, and ensure valid JSON/CSV formatting.

When done, ensure the outputs reflect exactly these rules and constraints.
